meta.title: Drift — Sculptural Sound Art
meta.description: Drift reimagines the white noise machine as sculptural sound art, exploring the continuum between noise and music through networked, resonant instruments.

header.subtitle: Sculptural sound art exploring the continuum between noise and music.

hero.title: The white noise machine, reimagined as art.
hero.introCopy: Drift transforms utilitarian white noise machines into networked, resonant sound sculptures. Using microcontrollers, actuators, and tuned materials, each sculpture slowly shifts from noise toward emergent musical patterns — inviting deep listening and reflection on sonic self-control.

noteCards.title: What you'll experience
noteCards.body: A dynamic soundscape among sculptures incorporating resonant materials that reflect both light and sound. Witness the transition from masking noise to emergent music.

noteCards.title: An interdisciplinary collaboration
noteCards.body: Sound design and programming by Tate Carson, design and fabrication by Tim Murray. The project integrates electronics and software with traditional woodcraft.

noteCards.title: The central question
noteCards.body: In what ways can the white noise machine be reconceived as an artistic object that illuminates the continuum between noise and music?

process.title: How the work came together.
process.description: Drift evolved through repeated cycles of prototyping, material testing, and sonic calibration. Each revision asked the same question: how can the sculpture remain visibly constructed — tool marks, seams, and all — while producing a transformed listening experience?

theory.title: How noise, sound, and listening shape daily life.
bulletList: Orphic Media: technologies like white noise machines modify perception and reshape mood and attention in ways we rarely examine.
Material memory: surfaces hold evidence of making, so process remains visible as part of the finished piece.
Threshold instrument: the sculpture gradually reveals the continuum between noise and music, turning a utilitarian object into a platform for reflective listening.

cards.title: Orphic Media
cards.body: Drift reframes the white noise machine as a technology that modifies perception and experience — emphasizing modulation over traditional representation.

cards.title: Noise as creative material
cards.body: The sculptures position noise not as something to mask or eliminate, but as creative material to be reshaped and experienced as music.

cards.title: Deep listening
cards.body: By shifting between noise and musical patterns, the work encourages attentive listening and expanded awareness of everyday sound environments.

cards.title: Location
cards.body: DSU Gallery, Dakota State University, Madison, SD.

cards.title: Duration
cards.body: Ideal dwell time: 2 to 5 minutes for a quick pass, longer for close listening.

cards.title: What to notice
cards.body: Listen for the slow transition from white noise to emergent musical patterns. Observe how resonant materials reflect both light and sound.

cards.title: Credits
cards.body: Sound design and programming: Tate Carson. Design and fabrication: Tim Murray.

quote.text: Drift encourages audiences to reconsider their listening habits and to recognize the artistry inherent in mediated sounds that influence daily life.

footer.text: Drift is supported by Dakota State University. The project integrates sound art scholarship with innovative artistic methods to expand discourse on underexamined sound technologies.
